title: Lesson 432
transcript: Here's a perfect example of where, you know, as we hear of things and we know that, for example, the exam and the industry is putting more emphasis on certain things. We want to alert you and get you more prepared. So here's a video that we created. because we've been seeing a lot more emphasis in the industry and on the exam on PAP, CHAP and EAP that we just talked about. And so you'll remember that these are authentication protocols that are really part of the PPP protocol. The PPP protocol that operates at layer two is really the foundation for all remote access. And as part of that, companies can configure it to provide the level of authentication that they need and want for people, for example, that are connecting in using remote access like VPN solutions and so on. Here's a slide that summarizes some of the really important information that we need to remember about these. PAP. very simple right we've we've listed these by order of really worst to best and PAP very simple authentication in fact we talked about how it only provides authentication by using a static reusable plain text password. So your password, for example, travels across the line in plain text and you can never change it. Companies cannot force you to change it, for example, every 30 days or 60 days or whatever. It becomes static, which obviously isn't very good at all, especially the part about sending your password across the line in plain text. That's never good, as you know. That's why CHAP is much better. challenge handshake authentication protocol. As you can see here, it takes care of some of the problems that PAP has and adds more security. So now we have the opportunity to start using, for example, two factor authentication and that's why this is a really good choice for a lot of organizations. Because it's more secure, you can support two-factor authentication and that gives us protection against replay protection and so on. So obviously that's much better and very popular in the industry. What's even better than this, as we said earlier, is EAP because of really the E, right? The E stands for extensible, which really means companies can massage it because there's different flavors of it to give companies the level of security and authentication that they want for their remote access. And in fact, we're gonna see a slide right after this one that talks about the different flavors and each of the things that get emphasized in each of those flavors. But we know that a lot of people are seeing a question on a specific flavor of EAP which is called Protected EAP. That's what PEAP stands for. And what you need to remember about this flavor of EAP is that it encapsulates the authentication information using a SSL slash TLS tunnel. That's what we've summarized for you. So PEAP, which stands for Protected EAP, encapsulates your authentication information across the tunnel using SSL and TLS. That's what protected EAP uses. Now here's a really nice chart of the different flavors that EAP actually has. So there's different ones that you see here. and depending on what your organization requires, like for example, if they want client authentication and or server authentication, and whether they want more than adequate security, for example, and support in the industry for things like VPN solutions and whether they want to stay with proprietary techniques that their vendors may be using. These are all options that companies can look at and then obviously pick the right flavor of EAP. Now the ones that we've highlighted here EAP TLS. Now don't confuse this with PEAP that we just talked about, right? PEAP that we just talked about is protected EAP and we said the authentication traffic For example, your user ID and password is protected using a SSL TLS tunnel. But EAP TLS is a little bit different. It actually uses a certificate for client authentication and a certificate for server authentication as well. And as you can see here, it provides really high security, high industry support. But again, and a lot of companies are looking for this, it's not proprietary. So you can massage it and use it with any basically remote access solution or VPN solution that you may be using. The other one that a lot of people are seeing on the exam is EAP-MD-5, and you'll recognize that, hopefully MD-5. That is a hashing algorithm that we discussed in the cryptography section, right? And anytime you see hashing algorithms, you know that they're used for integrity. All right. So that's what it focuses on, obviously, here. But it doesn't provide server authentication, low security, because the only thing that really it focuses on is integrity. All right. But if you see this. Just remember integrity, that's its purpose basically. But these are different flavors of EAP. And again, depending on what the company is looking for from the perspective of them offering remote access. they can massage their EAP solutions based on their requirements, which you see at the top here. But particularly remember, EAP TLS uses certificates to authenticate both the client and the server and provides really high security and it's non-proprietary and if you see EAP-MD5 just remember MD5 is a hashing algorithm which is focused on integrity protection.
